external.description: ArXiv, a popular online repository for scientific papers, is implementing a policy to ban authors who submit papers containing AI-generated errors. This is due to concerns about the misuse of AI tools and the resulting detrimental impact on academic integrity. The ban will last for one year and targets instances of "AI-generated slop" within the paper's content. Examples of such violations include hallucinated citations and meta-comments, indicating a lack of author review. Authors are responsible for ensuring the accuracy and integrity of their submissions, regardless of the tools used. The penalty applies when there is clear evidence that authors haven't checked the generated content. This policy is a one-strike rule, meaning a single violation results in the ban. After the ban, future submissions made to ArXiv require prior acceptance by a peer-reviewed venue. Decisions to impose the ban can be appealed, ensuring fairness and accommodating exceptional circumstances. The policy is enforced after evaluation by the moderator and confirmation from the Section Chair. This policy is designed to uphold the trustworthiness of the scientific research published on ArXiv.
external.title: ArXiv to Ban Researchers for a Year if They Submit AI Slop

🎙️ Charaktere & Cast (Japanisch)
»Alice Kuonji« von Kana Hanazawa
Eine Hexe, die in der modernen Welt im Verborgenen lebt. Sie besucht die Reien Girls’ Academy, eine bekannte Schule für junge Damen.
»Soujyuro Sizuki« von Yūsuke Kobayashi
Ein Junge vom Land, der in die moderne Welt gekommen ist. Eines Tages wechselt er an dieselbe Oberschule, die auch Aoko und die anderen besuchen.
»Aoko Aozaki« von Haruka Tomatsu
Eine Magierin, die in der heutigen Zeit lebt, allerdings noch als Lehrling. Seit ihrem Eintritt in die Oberschule soll sie als Magierin das Erbe ihres Großvaters antreten.

📜 Beschreibung 》
Ende der 1980er-Jahre wird Aoko Aozaki unerwartet zur Erbin einer alten Magierfamilie bestimmt, obwohl sie selbst kaum Interesse an Magie hat. Widerwillig beginnt sie ihre Ausbildung bei Alice Kuonji, einer jungen Hexe, die abgeschieden in einem Herrenhaus auf einem Hügel lebt. Zwischen Schulalltag, Schülervertretung und den Regeln der Magie gerät Aoko immer tiefer in eine Welt, die sie nie betreten wollte. Gleichzeitig taucht mit Sojuro Shizuki ein ungewöhnlicher Austauschschüler auf, während ihre ältere Schwester Toko Aozaki im Hintergrund eigene Pläne verfolgt.

🎙️ Charaktere & Cast (Japanisch)
»Takamichi Araki« von Taito Ban
Reiyas Sekretär. Er stammt aus einer Nebenlinie, die den jeweiligen Oberhäuptern der Kiryūin-Familie seit Generationen dient, und steht bereits seit seiner Kindheit in Reiyas Dienst.

»Ouga Kiyama« von Nobunaga Shimazaki
Der nächste Erbe der Kiyama-Familie, der wichtigsten Nebenlinie der Kiryūin-Familie, und Sakurakos älterer Bruder. Außerdem ist er Vizepräsident von Reiyas Firma.

»Sakurako Kiyama« von Aya Endō
Reiyas Verlobte. Innerhalb der Kiyama-Familie besitzt sie unter den Gleichaltrigen Reiyas die stärkste spirituelle Kraft und wurde deshalb als seine Verlobte ausgewählt.

📜 Beschreibung 》
In einem Japan, in dem Menschen und Ayakashi Seite an Seite leben, gilt es als größte Ehre, von einem mächtigen Wesen zur Braut erwählt zu werden. Für die unscheinbare Oberschülerin Yuzu Shinonome war dieses Glück jedoch immer unerreichbar, denn in ihrer Familie steht nur ihre jüngere Schwester Karin im Mittelpunkt, die Braut eines Dämonenfuchses. Tag für Tag wird Yuzu übersehen, verglichen und verletzt, bis ihr eines Tages auf einer Brücke ein Mann begegnet, dessen bloße Anwesenheit alles verändert. Sein Name ist Reiya Kiryuin, ein Oni an der Spitze der Ayakashi, und seine ersten Worte lassen Yuzus Welt erbeben: „Ich habe dich gefunden, meine Braut.“

"The photograph captures the radiant beauty of three common sunflowers (Helianthus annuus) in full bloom. Each sunflower boasts vibrant yellow petals that radiate outward from a central, dark brown disk. These petals form a circular arrangement, creating a striking contrast against the blurred gray background. The sunflowers are at different stages of opening, adding visual interest. Their petals curve gently, imbuing them with a sense of movement and liveliness. Within the dark centers, intricate patterns of small seeds spiral outward, following the natural Fibonacci sequence. These seeds contribute to the sunflowers’ exquisite appearance.

The sunflowers are attached to green stems, which provide sturdy support. These stems lead to broad green leaves, adding freshness and complementing the bright blooms. Against the blurred background, the sunflowers stand out, ensuring that the focus remains on their vivid colors and intricate details. Overall, the image evokes feelings of brightness, warmth, and natural elegance. For those with vision disabilities, imagining the vibrant yellow petals and contrasting dark centers can evoke a sense of natural beauty and joy." - Microsoft Copilot with edits

value.text:
GitHub Copilot moves to token based billing on June 1, and its users have now got a calculator that shows how much they've been actually burning every month - some $39-a-month users have spent $1500 to $5800 in tokens a month. Microsoft's subsidies are insane.

www.wheresyoured.at/premium-what...

images.alt:
So, for the uninitiated, the vast majority of AI startups — from search engines like Perplexity to legal software like Harvey to even Anthropic’s Claude subscriptions — do not bill users based on their actual token burn. 

When you search a query on Perplexity, Perplexity itself pays the per-million-token rate — for example, Claude Sonnet 4.6 costs $3 per million input tokens (about four characters) and $15 per-million output tokens (both the stuff it creates and the plans it makes to execute an output using chain-of-thought reasoning, which I explained here). You, the customer, pay a simple flat fee each month. 

As a result, you’re shielded from the actual cost of the prompt. You’re also shielded from the knowledge of the prompt’s cost.

Another good example is Microsoft’s AI coding tool GitHub Copilot, where users would get a certain amount of “premium requests” per month, with newer and more-powerful models costing more requests, and users able to buy more “premium requests” for a fee. As of June 1, 2026, Microsoft will move every GitHub Copilot subscriber to token-based billing, leading to users paying what their actual costs might be. 

To ease the transition — if you can call it that — Microsoft has created a calculator to show how much your current GitHub Copilot usage would cost if you were actually paying for tokens, and the results are brutal:

One user paying $10-a-month burned around $46.70 of tokens, and this is one of the least-gratuitous examples I could find.

value.text:
GitHub Copilot moves to token based billing on June 1, and its users have now got a calculator that shows how much they've been actually burning every month - some $39-a-month users have spent $1500 to $5800 in tokens a month. Microsoft's subsidies are insane.

www.wheresyoured.at/premium-what...

images.alt:
So, for the uninitiated, the vast majority of AI startups — from search engines like Perplexity to legal software like Harvey to even Anthropic’s Claude subscriptions — do not bill users based on their actual token burn. 

When you search a query on Perplexity, Perplexity itself pays the per-million-token rate — for example, Claude Sonnet 4.6 costs $3 per million input tokens (about four characters) and $15 per-million output tokens (both the stuff it creates and the plans it makes to execute an output using chain-of-thought reasoning, which I explained here). You, the customer, pay a simple flat fee each month. 

As a result, you’re shielded from the actual cost of the prompt. You’re also shielded from the knowledge of the prompt’s cost.

Another good example is Microsoft’s AI coding tool GitHub Copilot, where users would get a certain amount of “premium requests” per month, with newer and more-powerful models costing more requests, and users able to buy more “premium requests” for a fee. As of June 1, 2026, Microsoft will move every GitHub Copilot subscriber to token-based billing, leading to users paying what their actual costs might be. 

To ease the transition — if you can call it that — Microsoft has created a calculator to show how much your current GitHub Copilot usage would cost if you were actually paying for tokens, and the results are brutal:

One user paying $10-a-month burned around $46.70 of tokens, and this is one of the least-gratuitous examples I could find.
This guy spending $10-a-month burned $54.26 in tokens, and this one spent $23.81 in tokens. This guy spent $81 in tokens.
One user paying around $39-a-month burned $1357.16 of tokens.
One user who paid around $54.43 in a month burned $1,196.55 in tokens.
One user spending $39-a-month burned $204.55 in tokens.
One user spending $39-a-month burned $5,851.77 in tokens.
One user spending $39-a-month burned $595.83 in tokens.
One user spending $39-a-month burned $1,063.52.

Mishima: A Life in Four Chapters is a 1985 biographical drama film directed by Paul Schrader

Japanese author Yukio Mishima and his private militia took control of the Japan Self-Defense Forces base in Ichigaya. Mishima felt that a speech would be enough to convince the JSDF members to abolish Article 9 of the Japanese Constitution.

Sadly for Mishima, he declined to use a microphone so his speech was barely audible amongst the crowd (not helped by the presence of low-flying helicopters) and received jeers instead.

After the speech, Mishima would make himself faster by putting sword-holes in him.

實測發現，LLM 傾向自己完成所有工作，而不是將任務分派給子 agent。即便透過 prompt 強制建立層級結構，主 agent 也常會介入並取代子 agent 的工作。

現有工具如 Claude swarms，也未能完美解決這個問題。最終產出的合併結果，仍需主 agent處理，但其整合能力有待加強。

這顯示目前的 LLM 設計，本質上還是偏向單一執行者。要實現多 agent 的高效協作，可能需要從模型訓練階段就納入相關考量。

GitHub Copilot moves to token based billing on June 1, and its users have now got a calculator that shows how much they've been actually burning every month - some $39-a-month users have spent $1500 to $5800 in tokens a month. Microsoft's subsidies are insane.

www.wheresyoured.at/premium-what...

images.alt:
So, for the uninitiated, the vast majority of AI startups — from search engines like Perplexity to legal software like Harvey to even Anthropic’s Claude subscriptions — do not bill users based on their actual token burn. 

When you search a query on Perplexity, Perplexity itself pays the per-million-token rate — for example, Claude Sonnet 4.6 costs $3 per million input tokens (about four characters) and $15 per-million output tokens (both the stuff it creates and the plans it makes to execute an output using chain-of-thought reasoning, which I explained here). You, the customer, pay a simple flat fee each month. 

As a result, you’re shielded from the actual cost of the prompt. You’re also shielded from the knowledge of the prompt’s cost.

Another good example is Microsoft’s AI coding tool GitHub Copilot, where users would get a certain amount of “premium requests” per month, with newer and more-powerful models costing more requests, and users able to buy more “premium requests” for a fee. As of June 1, 2026, Microsoft will move every GitHub Copilot subscriber to token-based billing, leading to users paying what their actual costs might be. 

To ease the transition — if you can call it that — Microsoft has created a calculator to show how much your current GitHub Copilot usage would cost if you were actually paying for tokens, and the results are brutal:

One user paying $10-a-month burned around $46.70 of tokens, and this is one of the least-gratuitous examples I could find.
This guy spending $10-a-month burned $54.26 in tokens, and this one spent $23.81 in tokens. This guy spent $81 in tokens.
One user paying around $39-a-month burned $1357.16 of tokens.
One user who paid around $54.43 in a month burned $1,196.55 in tokens.
One user spending $39-a-month burned $204.55 in tokens.
One user spending $39-a-month burned $5,851.77 in tokens.
One user spending $39-a-month burned $595.83 in tokens.
One user spending $39-a-month burned $1,063.52.

As of June 1, 2026, Microsoft will move every GitHub Copilot subscriber to token-based billing, leading to users paying what their actual costs might be. 

To ease the transition — if you can call it that — Microsoft has created a calculator to show how much your current GitHub Copilot usage would cost if you were actually paying for tokens, and the results are brutal:

One user paying $10-a-month burned around $46.70 of tokens, and this is one of the least-gratuitous examples I could find.
This guy spending $10-a-month burned $54.26 in tokens, and this one spent $23.81 in tokens. This guy spent $81 in tokens.
